text:
With his brace yesterday, Harry Kane already scored 69 goals this season: the 2nd best of any player in the 21st century
emoji:n_stats: Stats
Harry Kane just equaled Cristiano Ronaldo's best goalscoring season ever and Messi's 2nd best goalscoring season ever, with 69 goals (club + country).

The top spot belongs - of course - to Messi, who scored a record 82 goals in the 2011-2012 season.

If, however, we consider Romario 2000's season, then the second place belongs to the brazilian, who scored 73 goals during that campaign.

Edit: For comparison purposes, as of today, Haaland scored 53 goals (his best is 56 in 2022-2023) and Mbappe 50 (his best is 53 in 2022-2023).

Edit 2: Even if we compare Kane's goalscoring season with players from the 1970s onwards (before that, the data starts to get really incomplete and even unreliable), he only has one other person ahead of him: Gerd Muller scored 73 goals in the 1972-1973 season.

Edit 3: Considering career official goals (senior top-tier clubs + national A teams), Harry Kane now sits in 24th place of all time, with 507 goals, leaving behind Hugo Sánchez (506). He's the first Englishman on the list.

Ahead of him only Roberto Dinamite (513), Karim Benzema (523), György Sárosi (530), Alfredo Di Stéfano (537), Jimmy McGrory (550), Uwe Seeler (552), Fernando Peyroteo (553), Imre Schlosser (553), Zlatan Ibrahimovi? (561), Glenn Ferguson (563), Eusébio (578), Luis Suárez (606), Abe Lenstra (624), Joe Bambrick (626), Gerd Müller (634), Jimmy Jones - the top British player on this list (639), Robert Lewandowski (697), Josef Bican (722), Ferenc Puskás (725), Romário (756), Pelé (762), Lionel Messi (914) and Cristiano Ronaldo (973).

As of today, Mbappé has 427 and Haaland has 354.
label: analysis
notes: Extensive statistical breakdown with comparisons, historical context, and verifiable data. This is a textbook analysis post.

text:
[MARCA] Michael Olise is Real Madrid's top target, and the club is considering making a mega-offer for the Bayern player. Real Madrid could raise their bid above €200M, with a limit of around €220M. This operation, Florentino Pérez's dream signing, is separate from that of Enzo Fernández.
emoji:n_transfer: Transfer News

At this point, it seems clear that the offer for Julián was a bluff , more aimed at complicating the Argentine's signing for Barcelona ( Florentino is being very belligerent with the Catalans in light of the 'Negreira case ') than being a genuine intention. 

It won't be easy. Bayern Munich isn't a selling club; quite the opposite. " If Florentino Pérez wants to send us an offer, which hasn't happened yet, he can save himself the trouble, " said Herbert Hainer , president of the German club, when Olise emerged as a potential target for Real Madrid following Florentino Pérez's announcement in 'Horizonte'. Real Madrid still has those 150 million euros safely tucked away, but it seems clear that taking Olise from the Allianz Arena will cost more.

Will that offer of over 200 million be enough? If there's one thing that's clear in football, it's that players play where they want . And Olise has a great supporter in Real Madrid. Not just any supporter. Kylian Mbappé , after becoming France's top scorer, had only words of praise for his right winger. " With Michael, it's super easy, because he's a player who always keeps his head up. With him, you just have to think about offering him a solution, a passing lane, and he sees it. He knew it, and it was really cool, " said the Real Madrid striker after the victory against Senegal , which opened up with a precise vertical pass from the Bayern player to set up the Madrid player. A natural and effective connection that was sorely missed last season at Real Madrid .

text:
16 teams at the World Cup include at least one French citizen, with 6 French captains
As a French fan watching the France - Senegal, I was amused that the only goal we conceded was both scored and assisted by French citizens (Ibrahim Mbaye and Iliman Ndiaye, respectively). I also noticed the equalizer that got DR Congo a point against Portugal was also both scored and assisted by French citizens (Yoane Wissa and Arthur Masuaku). Those four players were all born and raised in France.
I then looked deeper into the 112 French citizens playing at this World Cup, mostly using u/PiggySVW’s great work with their interactive map. I figured no less than16 teams have at least one French citizen, including 6 captains : Aďssa Mandi (Algeria), Johny Placide (Haiti), Kalidou Koulibaly (Senegal), Jordan Ayew (Ghana), Ryan Mendes (Cabo Verde), and obviously Kylian Mbappé.
Most of those players were born and raised in France, but not all of them. As you would expect, the non-French teams with the most French players are those of countries most represented among France’s second and third-generation immigrants, with six historically tied to France’s former colonial empire (Algeria, Morocco, Tunisia, Haiti, Côte d’Ivoire, Senegal). Four other African nations are represented (DR Congo, Ghana, Cabo Verde, Egypt), as well as five other countries with one single player who happens to be French for various reasons (Colombia, Germany, Qatar, Spain, USA).
Here are the 16 teams, ranked by number of French citizens, as well as their French citizens, starting with the obvious :
France (26): Akliouche, Barcola, Cherki, Dembélé, Digne, Doué, Gusto, L. Hernandez, T. Hernandez, Kanté, Konaté, Koné, Koundé, Lacroix, Maignan, Mateta, Mbappé (c), Olise, Rabiot, Risser, Saliba, Samba, Tchouaméni, Thuram, Upamecano, Zaďre-Emery
Algeria (13): Aďt-Nouri, Aouar, Bentaleb, Chaďbi, Chergui, Ghedjemis, Gouiri, Hadj Moussa, Hadjam, Mahrez, Mandi (c), Mastil, Zidane
Haiti (12): Bellegarde, Duverne, Expérience, Fortuné, Isidor, Joseph, Nazon, Paugain, Pierre, Placide (c), Providence, Simon
DR Congo (11): Bakambu, Banza, Batubinsika, Kakuta, Kalulu, Kapuadi, Masuaku, Mbuku, Moutoussamy, Mpasi-Nzau, Wissa
Côte d’Ivoire (11): Bonny, Doué, Fofana, Fofana, Guessand, Koné, Lafont, Ndicka, Operi, Pépé, Wahi
Senegal (11): Diarra, Diaw, Diouf, Gueye, Koulibaly (c), Mbaye, A. Mendy, E. Mendy, Ndiaye, Niakhaté, Sarr
Morocco (7): Bouaddi, Diop, El Aynaoui, El Mourabet, Halhal, Sbaď, Yassine
Tunisia (7): Achouri, Bronn, Gharbi, Hannibal, Skhiri (c), Talbi, Valery
Cabo Verde (5) : Costa, Da Costa, Mendes (c), Moreira, Semedo
Ghana (3) : Ayew (c), Owusu, Senaya
Colombia (1): Ospina
Egypt (1): Hassan
Germany (1): Sané
Qatar (1): Boudiaf
Spain (1): Laporte
USA (1): Weah.
Honorable mention: Brazil and PSG captain Marquinhos said a few months ago that he was applying for French citizenship after 13 years in Paris, with no public update since December.
Note that Guéla and Désiré Doué, who play for Côte d’Ivoire and France, are brothers. They both had their entire youth career at the Rennes academy and played together for the first team until 2024. Well, Lucas and Theo Hernandez are brothers too, though that’s less noteworthy. Besides, Algerian goalkeeper Luca Zidane’s father played for France.
So far, after all French players have played their first game, 9 have had a direct goal contribution. In chronological order: Hannibal (1A-TUN), Mbappé (2G-FRA), Olise (1A-FRA), Barcola (1G-FRA), Rabiot (1A-FRA), Mbaye (1G-SEN), Ndiaye (1A-SEN), Wissa (1G-COD), Masuaku (1A-COD). That’s a tally I’ll probably have fun updating at the end of the tournament.
Conversely, 5 French GKs have played and conceded a total of 9 goals : Placide (1-HAI), Mpasi-Nzau (1-COD), Mendy (3-SEN), Maignan (1-FRA) and Zidane (3-ALG), Yahia Fofana (CIV) has however kept a clean sheet against Ecuador, shout out to him.

In my opinion this is an attempt at creating a "hostile atmosphere" look at football and basketball in some eastern european countries, see the flares, the loudness. Do you think those fans are silent if a song is being played for the other team? No, they drown it out. Because they want the other team to feel unwelcome, and to give their own team an advantage in that regard.

People who see it as disrespectful in my opinion are probably more neutral fans who don't feel as strongly about their team. There is a big belief in football culture of being the "12th man" so being silent at a moment where you may give your team an advantage is just incorrect in that culture. The only exceptions to this are minutes of silence, or claps to show respect usually for somebody dying -- but even these things are not universally respected.

Anyway. I'm not justifying it, just explaining it. And it definitely isn't unique to England.
label: analysis
notes: Detailed analysis of football culture, explaining the "12th man" concept and why fans boo anthems. Provides reasoning, context, and even acknowledges exceptions. This is substantive cultural analysis.

text:
A Simple Guide to Group Standings & 3rd Place Qualification (And why you shouldn't overanalyze it)
Since the World Cup expanded to its massive new format, I’ve seen a lot of confusion around how the standings work. Some of our new friends from North America love to overanalyze every single mathematical scenario, but this system isn't actually new.

The World Cup used the exact same "best 3rd-placed teams" rule back in 1994 (ironically, when the US last hosted it), the only difference being that there were fewer groups back then (6 groups instead of the current format).

So, let’s break down exactly how the math works in plain English.

1. How to Read a Standings Table (The Columns)

When you open an app like Flashscore, you will see a row of letters next to a team's name. Here is what they mean:

MP (or GP): Matches Played. (Every team plays 3 games in the group stage).

W: Wins (Worth 3 points).

D: Draws / Ties (Worth 1 point each).

L: Losses (Worth 0 points).

G (or GF:GA): Goals scored vs Goals conceded (e.g., 4:1 means they scored 4 and conceded 1).

GD (+/-): Goal Differential. This is Goals Scored minus Goals Conceded. (If you score 4 and concede 1, your GD is +3 or 3).

PTS: Total Points. This is the most important number.

2. The Group Tie-Breakers (FIFA Rules vs. UEFA Rules)

A lot of people get confused here because they remember Qatar 2022 and older World Cups where goal difference was always the very first rule. In fact, for the first time since 1970, FIFA has officially changed its historical rule for this tournament and aligned with the UEFA style.

This means head-to-head records are now the primary tie-breaker instead of overall goal difference. They look at the actual match played between the tied teams first.

According to the official FIFA World Cup Regulations & Tie-Breakers, if two or more teams finish equal on group-stage Points (PTS), here is the exact official order used to separate them in the standings:

Head-to-Head Points: Most points obtained in the group matches played between the tied teams.

Head-to-Head Goal Differential: Superior goal difference in the group matches played between the tied teams.

Head-to-Head Goals Scored: Most goals scored in the group matches played between the tied teams.

Total Goal Differential (GD): Superior goal difference in all 3 group matches (this is where overall GD finally kicks in).

Total Goals Scored: Most goals scored in all 3 group matches.

Fair Play Points: A disciplinary system where you lose points for cards (-1 for yellow, -3 for indirect red, -4 for direct red).

FIFA World Ranking: If teams are tied on absolutely everything on the pitch and on cards, the team that enters the tournament with the higher official FIFA World Ranking automatically gets the higher spot. No more random luck or drawing names out of a hat!

What is the FIFA World Ranking? Think of it as a permanent, global leaderboard for international football that has been ongoing since 1992 and never resets. It uses a mathematical system where teams constantly accumulate or lose points after every single match based on the importance of the game and the strength of the opponent. At the World Cup, if two teams are tied on absolutely everything else, the one sitting higher on this historical, ever-evolving leaderboard gets the edge.

3. How the "Best 3rd-Placed Teams" are Determined

The best 8 teams that finish in 3rd place across all groups will also advance to the Knockout Rounds. To rank them against each other, FIFA creates a separate "virtual table" containing only the 3rd-placed teams.

Crucial Note on this Table:
As you can see in the standings app, this live table updates in real-time after every single match, but it is completely temporary right now. There are still plenty of matches left to be played in the group stage.

A team might look safe in the top 8 today because some groups have only played 1 match while others have played 2, but the table will shift drastically. Do not panic or celebrate until every single group has finished all 3 rounds!

Since these teams obviously didn't play against each other, the tie-breakers for this specific table are slightly shorter:

Total Points

Total Goal Differential

Total Goals Scored

Fair Play Points (fewer cards)

FIFA World Ranking

4. How are the 3rd-placed teams assigned to Group Winners?

Since only 8 of the 12 group winners will face a 3rd-placed team (specifically the winners of Groups A, C, D, E, G, I, K, and L), FIFA created a preset master chart in the tournament regulations to handle this automatically.

As you can see in the official tournament schedule app above, teams like E1, I1, or D1 don't have a fixed opponent yet. Instead, they have a list of options in parentheses (like A3/B3/C3/F3).

Depending on the exact combination of the 8 groups that send their 3rd-placed teams through, that official FIFA chart automatically dictates the matchups. It’s a fixed puzzle where the pieces fall into place on their own once the group stage ends. No manual choices, no complicated math during the bracket stage!

The Bottom Line: Don't Stress!

Historically, if a team manages to get 4 points (e.g., 1 win and 1 draw), they are 99% qualified, regardless of whether they finish 2nd or 3rd in their group.

At the end of the day, if we spend all our time overanalyzing spreadsheets, calculating goal differentials after every whistle, and stressing over disciplinary points, we completely miss the point. Nowadays, they keep inventing all these meaningless metrics like xG (Expected Goals), advanced data analytics, and other overcomplicated stats that, in my opinion, are just ruining the beautiful game.

We are turning football into a math exam. Football is not a spreadsheet, It’s about the people in the stands, the last-minute winners, the heartbreaks, and the beautiful chaos. You don't need a degree in advanced mathematics or data science to enjoy a tournament. Just switch off the calculator, ignore the stats, cheer for your country, and let the game happen!
label: analysis
notes: Extremely detailed, informative post explaining the World Cup format, tie-breakers, and qualification rules. This is educational content with specific rules, examples, and reasoning. The author clearly did research. Note: Even though the conclusion says "don't overanalyze," the post itself is pure analysis.

text:
2026 FIFA World Cup Group C Permutations - BRA MAR HAI SCO
1ST:

Brazil needs a draw against Scotland if Morocco doesn’t win against Haiti
Brazil will require a win if Morocco beats Haiti and it will come down to group GD.

Morocco must beat Haiti and hope that Brazil doesn’t win or it will come down to group GD if Brazil wins.

Scotland must beat Brazil and hope that Morocco doesn’t win.

2ND:

Brazil will finish second if they draw Scotland and Morocco beats Haiti.
They can also finish second if they lose to Scotland and Morocco loses to Haiti and Brazil has a better group GD.

Morocco will finish second if they draw against Haiti and Brazil and doesn’t beat Scotland.
They will also finish second if both them and Brazil win and Brazil has a better group GD.

Morocco can lose to Haiti regardless of the other result but if Scotland wins, Morocco will need to have a better group GD than Brazil

.Scotland will finish second if they beat Brazil and Morocco beats Haiti.

3RD: (And possible advancement)

Brazil will finish third on 4 POINTS if they lose to Scotland and Morocco doesn’t lose to Haiti.

Morocco will finish third on 4 POINTS if they lose to Scotland beats Brazil and Morocco has a worse group GD than Brazil.

Scotland will finish third on 3 POINTS if they don’t beat Brazil. If Scotland finishes level on points with Morocco they lose head to head.

Haiti is eliminated as they can only finish level on points with Scotland and they have lost the head to head against them
label: analysis
notes: Detailed breakdown of all possible qualification scenarios for Group C. Uses logical reasoning to explain what each team needs. Pure analysis.

text:
ELI5: Why is Haiti eliminated from the Group Stage and cannot possibly advance to the Group of 32?
I just watched the Brazil vs. Haiti match and now they're saying that Haiti has been eliminated even though there is still one more game to be played in the group stage.
The current group C standings are:
Brazil 4
Morocco 4
Scotland 3
Haiti 0
Of course, it is mathematically impossible for Haiti to get into 1st or 2nd place. But theoretically Haiti could defeat Morocco by a large margin and Brazil could defeat Scotland, resulting in:
Brazil 7
Morocco 4
Haiti 3 (theoretically their GD could be higher than Scotland's)
Scotland 3
My understanding is that 8 of the 12 groups will have their 3rd place team advance, and the criteria for deciding those 8, in order, are:
Points (there are currently 0 other groups that have a team in 3rd place that has 3 points or more, and 8 groups get to bring their 3rd place team along)
Goal difference
Number of goals scored
Fair play score
FIFA rankings
So of course this is all theoretical and unlikely. But if Haiti ended up beating Morocco by 10 goals, and Scotland lost to Brazil, please explain to me like I'm 5 why Haiti could not advance to the group of 32 as a third place team?
Please correct me if I have any of this wrong.
EDIT: Turns out I was wrong on what the tiebreaker was. Since Scotland beat Haiti, Haiti will be in 4th place in the group no matter what now, even in the above (unlikely) scenario.
label: analysis
notes: Asks a thoughtful question and provides detailed reasoning about why Haiti is eliminated. Explains the math and tie-breaker rules. Even though it's framed as a question, it contains analysis of the rules and scenarios.

text: Think of it like this: you can't apply for the 'best 3rd-place team' spots until your own group says you finished 3rd. Haiti's problem isn't the big 12-group comparison. Their problem is Scotland is blocking the door inside Group C.
label: analysis
notes: Clear, reasoned explanation using an analogy. Helps explain the qualification rules. Informative.

text: You missed the main tiebreaker - head-to-head. It is impossible for a team that lost to another team to surpass said team on any other tiebreaker. So, given they'd be tied if Haiti wins and Scotland loses on matchday 3, Scotland is guaranteed a higher spot against Haiti, regardless of the specific results on that matchday.
label: analysis
notes: Corrects the original post's misunderstanding with clear explanation of the head-to-head tiebreaker rule. Informative and precise.

text:
as football fans we need to talk about how unfair FIFA is

The current unfair allocation:

Confederation (Region)	Total Countries (FIFA Members)	Direct Slots (2026)	Regional Success Rate (%)
UEFA (Europe)	55	16	29.09%
CAF (Africa)	54	9	16.67%
AFC(Asia)	45	7	15.56%
CONMEBOL (South America)	10	6	60.00%
CONCACAF (North & Central America)	35	6	17.14%
OFC(Ocenia)	12	2	16.67%
Total / Overall	211	46*	21.80%
For people not good with stats, the above table implies ASIA and AFRICA got the worst deal. If we go by population, it will be far far worse and unfair.

Everyone shall reflect how wrong FIFA is.
Why does LATAM have 6 spots for 10 nations while Asia has same count for 45 nations?

People talking about that 32 -> 48 AFC gained 3 spots, but then so did LATAM 2 spots and EU 3 spots when they never needed it. AFC and CAF were under represented and they should have got those 5-8 spots. FIFA threw token breadcrumbs to AFC and said "deal with it" we don't want you in WC as our fans prefer to watch EU/LATAM team only and it is all fair and inclusive.

The actual fair allocation below inclusive of host nations:

Africa: 12 seats for 54 nations

Asia: 10 seats for 45 nations

Europe and all those islands colonies: 13 seats for 55 nations

North America, South America, Aus/NZ/Oceania and everybody else: 13 seats of remaining 57 nations

If one stands against the critics of compositions of squad, one should also stand against the unfair representation. FIFA is prejudiced against Asia and Africa, typical colonial era mindset.
How come the "business reasons" become suddenly more important than representation?

No way you are telling me that a Ecuadorian deserves representation more than a Thai.
label: analysis
notes: Comprehensive analysis of FIFA's slot allocation with specific numbers, comparisons, and arguments about fairness. The author uses data (the table) and makes a reasoned case about representation. This is substantive analysis, even though it's opinionated.

text: In my opinion, each confederation should have a fixed percentage proportional to its recent history (the last 20 years) and a variable percentage proportional to its current state. The fixed percentage proportional to recent history could be linked to the level of football professionalization within the confederation. Obviously, Europe and South America have a huge advantage here, but it would force other confederations/countries to professionalize players, improve infrastructure, and increase revenue. The variable percentage could be based on performance in the previous World Cup (numerical weighting) + performance in friendly matches (since there is no other way to cross paths with other confederations)
label: analysis
notes: Makes a reasoned proposal for a new slot allocation system. Explains the logic behind fixed and variable percentages, provides reasoning about professionalization, and suggests specific metrics (previous World Cup performance, friendly matches). This is substantive analysis with a clear proposal.
